112. In light of the jurisprudence, the Board of Appeal finds it necessary to assess whether the CPVO fully
discharged its duty of diligence before registering the transfer of the Queen PVR, and in particular deleting
and entering the relevant particulars on the Register regarding the Queen PVR. In light of the relevant
legal framework and guidance, the Board of Appeal notes the following initial points in this regard.
113. Owners of PVRs have freedom as regards their transfer and passing on. They, in their discretion, are able
to determine if and how a PVR is transferred in accordance with relevant law. As such, the legal transfer
of a PVR is an issue primarily governed, in the first instance, by the legal rules and requirements applicable
under relevant national Member State and/or other law. Where disputes arise, it is generally an issue for
national justice systems, in the first instance, to resolve. The CPVO has not, per se, been afforded legal
powers or competencies to regulate, dictate and/or determine how a PVR right is legally transferred.
Moreover, as a general principle, the CPVO is not, per se, required to rule on contractual or legal questions
arising under national law in determining transfer of a PVR right (see, to that effect and by analogy,
General Court Judgment of 9 September 2011, David Chalk v Office for Harmonisation in the Internal
Market (CRAIC), Case T-83/09, EU:T:2011:450, paragraph 27). As recognised in, for example, EUIPO
guidelines concerning trademarks, where concerns may arise as to the legal ownership of a PVR – it would
generally be for the relevant national justice system(s), in the first instance, to address the legality and
lawfulness of a transfer and legal ownership of the PVR itself (see “Trade Mark Guidelines, Part E Register
Operations, Section 3 EUTMS and RCDS as objects of property, Chapter 1 Transfer, 4 requirements for
an application for registration of a transfer, Paragraph 3.5 ‘Proof of transfer’ which states “[…] If doubts
arise, the national courts deal with the legality of the transfer itself”).
114. At the same time, it cannot be overlooked, that the CPVO has been afforded exclusive legal powers and
competencies to implement and manage the EU PVR system. A critical component of the EU PVR system
is the Register. The Register, and the information it contains, is essential in ensuring the EU PVR system
functions as legally intended. Moreover, it is essential in maintaining the credibility and confidence in the
PVR system itself. The CPVO alone has been entrusted with ensuring the Register, and the information
contained within it, is correct and up to date. Of the information contained in the Register, data concerning
the ownership of a PVR is particularly important. Recording the correct transfer of ownership of a PVR
from one natural or legal person to another, and keeping the Register up to date in this regard, is a core
task of the CPVO of essential importance to the EU PVR system. As the custodian of the Register, it is
therefore incumbent upon the CPVO to ensure it takes all proportionate steps to ensure the information
contained in the Register regarding a transfer, is correct and can be relied upon.
115. It follows from the above, that before registering a transfer of a PVR in the Register, and in accordance
with its duty of diligence, it is incumbent on the CPVO to ensure, amongst other things: (1) that the CPVO
has the necessary information it needs to change the relevant details in the Register (e.g. the names of
the transferor and transferee, date, name of PVR, name and contact details of the relevant individuals or
persons acting as representatives to companies, etc); and (2) it has the necessary and sufficient
documentary evidence proving the transfer in law and fact (e.g. signed deeds / agreements / contracts
to transfer the PVR etc) - and supporting documentation (e.g. signed deeds / agreements / contracts
appointing representatives to represent a company or legal entity, and extracts from (business) registers
or official documents signed by relevant independent witnesses proving an individual has the right to
represent a company or legal entity). Moreover, when assessing the information and documentary
evidence available, it is settled case-law that the CPVO must take into account all the relevant factual and
legal aspects of the individual case (see, Marina Yachting, paragraph 58 and the case-law cited therein).
As such, the CPVO must consider, amongst other things, whether there are any grounds for a potential
concern. Where such grounds exist, it is incumbent on the CPVO to take further or additional steps to
resolve and clarify those issues before changes to the Register are made. This may include, as stated in
the EUIPO Guidelines referred to above at paragraph [104], obtaining “additional proof” of the transfer
and/or representation of a legal entity.
116. In light of the above, the Board of Appeal finds it necessary to consider whether the CPVO discharged its
duty of diligence before changing the details in the Register concerning the Queen PVR, taking into
account the pertinent facts and the pleas and arguments brought forward by the Parties.
117. As stated above at paragraph [4], on 23 September 2021, the CPVO advised Mr Renán that, in order for
the CPVO to change the Register to reflect the apparent transfer of the Queen PVR – the CPVO “Option
B” form concerning the “Assignment of (an) application(s) or (a) registered Community Plant Variety
Right(s)”7 should be used. As stated above at paragraphs [7-9], on 29 September 2021, Mr Renán then
7 https://cpvo.europa.eu/sites/default/files/documents/assignment_template_en_.pdf
17

provided an Option B form containing certain information (“Option B Form”). As also stated above at
paragraph [23], on 14 December 2021 the CPVO confirmed that the registration of the transfer of
ownership “was registered on the basis of the document provided on 29 September 2021 (model ES —
option B)”.
118. In these appeal proceedings, the CPVO has asserted that because Mr Renán provided the CPVO the Option
B Form containing certain information, Mr Renán was not required to provide the CPVO with “any [other]
additional documentary evidence”. The CPVO has stated that “…a duly completed and signed transfer
form from the [CPVO] constitutes a perfectly valid instrument to prove the assignment for which
registration is sought”. The CPVO takes the view that this approach is consistent with Article 23 of the
Basic Regulation and Article 79 of the IRR which, according to the CPVO, require a written document
bearing a digital or other signature. Should the CPVO require other information or documentation this
would, according to the CPVO, “be tantamount to creating de facto requirements that are exogenous to
the applicable legislation”. The CPVO has stated that requiring stakeholders to provide documentary
evidence that, for example, a specific individual has been validly appointed as a representative of a legal
entity, is not in accordance with the EU legislature’s intent and that requiring this type of documentary
evidence “…would even be contra legem, to the extent that the EU legislator wanted to design a system
with a relatively “low” evidentiary threshold, simple and general, which in any case would not be onerous".
119. The Board of Appeal finds that these arguments cannot succeed and should be rejected.
120. First, and as stated above at paragraph [111], the CPVO must act in accordance with the duty of diligence
in proceedings concerning the transfer of a PVR, and before registering a transfer. If a person were able
to legally dictate and automatically require the CPVO to register a transfer of a PVR in the Register merely
by, for example, providing a CPVO template form containing certain information with a digital or other
signature, it would impede on the CPVO’s ability to comply with the CPVO’s duty of diligence, and call into
question the ability for the CPVO to ensure the information in the Register is accurate, up-to-date, and
complete - and able to be relied upon.
121. Second, pursuant to the duty of diligence, it is incumbent on the CPVO to take all relevant and
proportionate steps to ensure it fully discharges that duty before changing the details of ownership of a
PVR in the Register. Although the CPVO may in its discretion determine the precise steps to be taken to
discharge the duty of diligence – the Board of Appeal notes that, in principle, the CPVO should ensure,
amongst other things, that the documentary evidence provided is up-to-date and complete, and does not
call into question or raise clear concerns or doubts regarding, for example: (1) the transfer of a PVR,
and/or (2) the ability for an individual to represent a legal entity during the CPVO proceedings in question.
As such, in this case, it was generally incumbent on the CPVO to ensure that the documentary evidence
was up-to-date and complete, and did not raise clear concerns or doubts concerning: (1) the
apparent/purported transfer of the Queen PVR from the Appellant to SGI, and (2) the ability of Mr Renán
to represent the Appellant and the SGI in CPVO proceedings concerning the transfer of the Queen PVR
from the Appellant.
122. Third, Article 79 of the IRR requires that the transfer of PVRs shall be entered in the Register “on the
production of” certain documentary evidence. It is therefore for the CPVO to determine what precise
documentary evidence the relevant persons must produce and provide the CPVO, in light of all the facts
and legal aspects of an individual case, so as to enable the CPVO to update and change the Register. The
CPVO must exercise its discretionary powers in accordance with EU law, including the duty of diligence
and the principle of proportionality. Where there is a heightened concern or risk due, for example, to a
potential conflict of interest – it may be necessary for the CPVO to take additional steps (including
obtaining additional documentation or assurances from the relevant parties) to discharge its duty of
diligence before registering a transfer.
123. Fourth, the primary legal intent of the CPVO template Option B form must be clearly understood in its
legal context. The primary legal intent of the CPVO template Option B form is not to ensure that parties
are able to transfer a PVR in all national jurisdictions throughout the European Union, the European
Economic Area and/or beyond. Whether, in a specific situation – such as the one in this case – a particular
document based on the Option B template and containing certain information – is legally capable of
transferring a PVR under relevant national law and has legally done so, is, in the first instance, for the
national justice systems to determine. As such, whether the Option B Form as provided by Mr Renán is
capable or transferring, and/or did transfer – on its own and/or in light of other documentation and facts
– the Queen PVR is a question primarily for the Spanish national justice system to determine. Nonetheless,
the primary intention of the CPVO Option B form itself, and the primary objective of stakeholders filling
in this template form and providing it to the CPVO – is to facilitate the CPVO in deciding whether, in
18

accordance with its duty of diligence, the CPVO is able to change the relevant details on the Register. As
stated above at paragraph [113], the CPVO has not, per se, been afforded legal powers or competencies
to regulate or dictate how a PVR right is legally transferred under national Member State or other law. As
such, a completed Option B form must be generally regarded as a document intended primarily to facilitate
the CPVO in understanding and examining the relevant facts and evidence in accordance with the duty of
diligence, and to help the CPVO ascertain whether it is legally possible for the CPVO to register the transfer
in the Register – not as a document primarily intended to effectuate the legal transfer of a PVR under,
for example, Spanish national law.
124. Lastly, as stated above, the intention of the legislature was to establish a Register, forming part of an EU
PVR system, which stakeholders could trust and rely upon. The CPVO’s role in ensuring the information
contained within the Register is accurate, up-to-date and reliable is therefore critical. Taking proportionate
steps and ensuring stakeholders provide relevant documentary evidence to ensure the system is not
subject to abuse cannot be regarded as inconsistent with the intention of the legislature or the CPVO’s
duty of diligence. In this regard, the Board of Appeal notes that the CPVO itself has more recently required
the Appellant to provide certain documentary evidence in order to effectuate the registration of the
transfer of the PVR back to the Appellant. This includes, for example, documentation concerning
“appointment of members of the board of directors of the company Angel Teresa Hermanos S.A., which
you have provided us with the signatures of the interested parties. - An extract from the Mercantile Registry
stating the change in the company's administrative body” - see paragraph [15] above. The Board of Appeal
note that it could be regarded as inconsistent to, on the one hand, assert that the CPVO is not required
to obtain this information and documentary evidence (and assert that to do so would be inconsistent with
the legislature’s intention) and, on the other hand, require that same or similar information and
documentary evidence in order to effectuate the registration of the transfer of the PVR back to the
Appellant company.
125. It follows from the above that merely because Mr Renán had provided an Option B Form containing certain
information bearing a digital stamp – this did not mean the CPVO was able, or required, to automatically
update the details of the Register concerning the ownership of the Queen PVR. Instead, it was incumbent
on the CPVO to understand, amongst other things, whether: (1) the information in this document was
correct, reliable, and complete – or whether, for example, it gave rise to a potential concern; and/or (2)
whether the information in this document (including the information on the apparent transfer of the PVR)
was sufficiently substantiated and supported by documentary evidence.
126. Given the points set out above, the Board of Appeal initially concludes that the duty of diligence applied
in this case. Receipt of the Option B Form by the CPVO did not mean that the CPVO was discharged from
its duty of diligence or that this requirement had been satisfied. Instead, it was incumbent on the CPVO,
in accordance with its duty of diligence, to examine carefully and impartially all the relevant factual and
legal aspects of the individual case and, amongst other things, to: (1) check the information it had
received in the Option B Form and otherwise, (2) ascertain whether it had all the information necessary
to effectuate the registration of the transfer, and (3) then ensure it had all the necessary and sufficient
documentary evidence.
127. In addition to the pleas and arguments summarised above, the Board of Appeal notes that the CPVO has
also put forward arguments asserting that, at the time the CPVO registered the transfer of the Queen
PVR in the Register, the CPVO was able to rely on a ‘presumption of validity’ pursuant to which the CPVO
could presume there had been a valid transfer of the Queen PVR. According to the CPVO, the name and
contact details of the individual apparently representing the transferor and transferee legal entities in this
case had already been inserted into the CPVO IT systems as a representative of the transferor company.
The individual was known to the CPVO and had been acting on behalf of the Appellant in the past as
regards the PVR in question vis-à-vis the payment of annual fees etc. And the CPVO had documentation
to support a presumption of validity.
128. The Board of Appeal finds that these arguments cannot succeed and should be rejected.
129. First, and as stated above, it is settled case-law that before registering a transfer the CPVO is under a
duty of diligence. As such, in this case, the CPVO was required to discharge that duty before registering
the transfer. Any potential presumption of validity cannot be regarded as negating the duty of diligence,
or as rendering the duty of diligence as null and void.
130. Second, the CPVO has not brought forward sufficient proof that a presumption of validity could exist in
this case. As clarified at the oral hearing, Mr Renán is not, and was not at the time he worked for and
represented the Appellant company, a lawyer, an accountant or other professional – and therefore was
19

not, per se, required to comply with a set of professional or ethical rules such as a duty to act in good
faith. Also, and as set out in more detail at paragraphs [131-134] below, in this case there was reason to
believe Mr Renán may have a personal vested interest in registering the transfer which might override
ethical, moral, professional or other considerations. To the extent that the CPVO takes the view that – as
Mr Renán was a director or officer of the Appellant company – the CPVO could presume that Mr Renán
was bound by a fiduciary duty to act in the best interests of the Appellant company, and therefore that
Mr Renán acted in a bona fide manner; the Board of Appeal notes that this pre-supposes that Mr Renán
was a director or officer of the company at the time Mr Renán submitted the request to register the
transfer. It was therefore all the more important for the CPVO to take proportionate steps to ascertain,
with sufficient and up-to-date supporting documentary evidence, that: (1) Mr Renán was a natural person
currently serving as an director, officer or other relevant person for the Appellant company and (2) Mr
Renán provided up-to-date documentary proof that he served the Appellant as a current director and
officer, and had the express legal right and authorisation from the Appellant company to represent it in
important proceedings before the CPVO involving the transfer of the Queen PVR to a third party. The
Board of Appeal addresses this issue at paragraphs [135- 149] below.
131. Lastly, insofar as any presumption of validity could have initially existed – that presumption did not exist
after Mr Renán had provided the Option B Form on 29 September 2021. That was because the Option B
Form provided by Mr Renán made clear that Mr Renán was seeking to act alone and represent two
separate legal entities (companies) at the same time, where the two companies in question had, prima
facie, conflicting interests.
132. As is clear, and as the CPVO has correctly pointed out in its written submissions during this appeal case,
a transfer of a PVR, and the rights associated with a PVR, is generally to the detriment of the transferor
and to the benefit of the transferee. This means that the interests of the transferor and transferee are
therefore not aligned – but conflict. Natural persons seeking to act for two separate legal persons, where
one is the transferor and the other the transferee, are therefore themselves – most likely to be conflicted
and unable to act unless additional explanation and safeguards are put in place. It may, for example, be
possible to address and resolve potential conflicts if there is a transfer between affiliates within the same
corporate family (e.g. transfers as between parent/holding companies and their subsidiaries, or as
between twin/sister legal entities) and safeguards are put in place (e.g. additional assurances, from
additional natural persons, along with evidential proof from the entities involved). However, in the absence
of any information, assurances, or documentary evidence that there is no conflict of interest and/or no
relevant concern – the Board of Appeal finds that it is not possible to invoke or rely upon a ‘presumption
of validity’ or ‘presumption of good faith’.
133. In this case, the information provided in the Option B Form on 29 September 2021 clearly indicated an
actual or potential conflict of interest existed. Despite this clear potential concern, the CPVO failed to
obtain additional information, assurances or documentary evidence to address the issue. Where there is
a clear potential conflict of interest, and where no action is taken to address or resolve that obvious
concern – it is self-evident that the CPVO cannot seek to invoke or rely on a purported presumption of
validity to leave the issue unresolved. Instead, in such situations, the CPVO must comply all the most
strictly with its duty of diligence and take further steps to address and resolve the apparent conflict of
interest in question. This is consistent with the duty of diligence to examine carefully and impartially all
the relevant factual and legal aspects of the individual case.
134. Given the above points, the Board of Appeal initially concludes that it was incumbent on the CPVO to fully
discharge its duty of diligence in this case by taking into account all the relevant factual and legal aspects
of the individual case (see, Marina Yachting, paragraph 58 and the case-law cited therein). The CPVO
could not rely upon a presumption of validity as, amongst other things, there were clear and unresolved
concerns - in particular a concern regarding a conflict of interest. Moreover, the Board of Appeal initially
concludes that by not addressing issues of clear concern – such as the apparent conflict of interest, and
by registering the transfer before these clear concerns were resolved – the CPVO did not act in accordance
with is duty of diligence. By, for example, not taking steps to address or resolve the issue of concern by
obtaining “additional proof” or assurances that there was no conflict of interest, or that safeguards had
been put in place to address the apparent conflict – the Board of Appeal initially concludes that the CPVO
acted in breach of this duty.
135. In addition to the pleas and arguments summarised above, the Board of Appeal notes that the CPVO has
also brought forward arguments asserting that, in this case, the CPVO had received documentation which
was sufficient to permit the registration of the transfer.
20

136. Before assessing these arguments in detail, the Board of Appeal recalls the following points. Firstly, and
has stated above (see paragraph [111]), the CPVO must adhere to, and fully discharge, its duty of
diligence in proceedings concerning the registration of a transfer. Secondly, and as also stated above
(see paragraph [115]), pursuant to the duty of diligence it is incumbent on the CPVO to ensure it has the
necessary and sufficient documentary evidence to register a transfer. As regards the precise nature and
scope of the documentary evidence required to register a transfer, this will depend on the factual and
other circumstances surrounding a particular case. However, and in any event, the Board of Appeal notes
that the CPVO must take proportionate steps to ensure the documentary evidence is, as stated at
paragraph [121] above, up-to-date and complete, and does not call into question or raise clear concerns
or doubts regarding, for example: (1) the transfer of a PVR, and/or (2) the ability for an individual to
represent a legal entity during the CPVO proceedings in question.
137. As regards the documentation concerning representation of the legal entities, the Board of Appeal takes
the view that it is particularly important for the CPVO to ensure that the documentary evidence does not
raise clear concerns and doubts regarding the natural person representing the transferor entity. That is
because, following the registration of the transfer, the transferor entity will likely lose its ability to
legitimately claim the benefits associated with ownership of the PVR. As such, and taking these general
points into consideration, the Board of Appeal notes the following.
138. Firstly, in this case, Mr Renán was appointed as a representative of the Appellant company, for certain
CPVO procedures, pursuant to a Procedural Representative Form signed and completed by Mr Renán on
12 April 2006. Mr Renán submitted the request for the CPVO to change the Register on 29 September
2021. The relevant documentation in place – was therefore more than 15 years old. Taking into
consideration the importance of having up-to-date documentation in place concerning the representation
of the transferor company, and taking into consideration the fact that the CPVO has not brought forward
sufficient reasons to justify the apparent absence of such up-to-date documentation in this case – the
Board of Appeal concludes that the documentation in place in this regard was not sufficiently up-to-date.
139. Secondly, the Procedural Representative Form of 12 April 2006 completed and signed by Mr Renán states,
amongst other things:
21

140. The Board of Appeal notes, in particular, that the form states that Mr Renán was the representative “con
relación a; mi solicitud de una protección Comunitaria de las obtenciones vegetales; titularidad de una
protección Comunitaria de las obtenciones vegetales”. It therefore follows that this form only appointed
Mr Renán to represent the Appellant before the CPVO insofar as the proceedings related to: (1) the
Appellant’s application for the Queen PVR; and (2) the Appellant’s ownership of the Queen PVR. That is
to say that the form only seemingly intended to appoint Mr Renán as the representative of the Appellant
company in proceedings before the CPVO insofar as those proceedings served to preserve and protect
the Appellant’s ownership of the PVR and not for other proceedings - such as the registered transfer from
the Appellant to a third party.
141. In this regard the Board of Appeal notes that on 11 January 2016 the CPVO adopted a new template
document concerning the representation of legal persons by natural individuals.8 The new template
document was revised to include within it, amongst other things, new and additional legal wording which
specifically addressed situations concerning the representation of legal persons in CPVO proceedings
regarding the loss of title/ownership of a PVR – including, therefore, CPVO proceedings regarding the
registration of a transfer of a PVR. In particular, the current and up-to-date version of the template
Procedural Representative Form in Spanish states:
142. The Board of Appeal notes, in particular, that the new template form therefore currently states that a
representative is appointed in respect of: “Todos los procedimientos (incluyendo cesiones de titulos)
con relación a mi(s)/nuestro(s) solicitud(es) y derecho(s). Todos los procedimientos (incluyendo
cesiones de titulos) con relación a mi(s)/nuestro(s) solicitud(es) y derecho(s) a las siguientes especies
8 https://cpvo.europa.eu/en/news-and-events/news/new-designation-procedural-representative-form
22

[…]” (emphasis added). The Board of Appeal notes, therefore, that the template form was revised by the
CPVO to include legal wording which the CPVO thought sufficiently important to necessitate revision of,
and inclusion in, the new template form. The Board of Appeal also notes that, in this case, the CPVO did
not require the Appellant and/or Mr Renán to provide a new document, based on the new template.
Moreover, the CPVO did not require the Appellant to provide a new document based on the new template,
even though the new legal wording included in the new template document regarding cessation of title,
was directly relevant to the CPVO procedure in question (i.e. registration of a transfer).
143. As such, and in short, on the basis of the documentation made available to the Board of Appeal, it appears,
in this case, that the documentation in place regarding representation of the Appellant consisted of a
document based on an old and outdated template and which omitted important legal wording. The
omission of that legal wording raises a clear concern and doubt as to whether Mr Renán has ever been
appointed to represent the Appellant company in proceedings concerning the registration of a transfer of
the Queen PVR to third parties. From the documentation provided to the Board of Appeal, it is not
apparent that the Appellant company has ever appointed Mr Renán as a representative before the CPVO
as regards procedures involving the transfer or assignment of the Queen PVR to third parties. The Board
of Appeal therefore concludes that the documentation in place in this regard was not sufficiently robust
or complete and could not be relied upon to register the transfer.
144. Lastly, the Board of Appeal note that the sufficiency and completeness of documentary evidence
concerning representation – and enabling the registration of a transfer – should be considered in light of
all the facts and circumstances. In this case, the Board of Appeal note, amongst other things, that: (1)
the natural person purporting to represent the transferor legal entity (i.e. Mr Renán) – had apparently,
on the basis of the available documentation – appointed himself to act on behalf of the transferor legal
entity (i.e. he/she had not been appointed to act for the company by different people working for or on
behalf of the transferor legal entity), (2) the natural person purporting to represent the transferor legal
entity (i.e. Mr Renán) was also seeking to act on behalf of the transferee company and therefore in a
situation where a conflict of interest apparently existed where personal interests could override ethical or
professional obligations, and (3) no other natural person working for, or on behalf of, the transferor legal
entity had, on the basis of the available documentation, apparently consented or approved of the transfer.
In these circumstances, the Board of Appeal notes that it was all-the-more important for the CPVO to
ensure the documentary evidence of valid and current representation of transferor legal entity by Mr
Renán - was sufficiently up-to-date and complete. As the CPVO has not brought forward relevant evidence
to prove that the documentary evidence was sufficient to permit registration of the transfer in these
circumstances, the Board of Appeal concludes that there was an absence of such documentary evidence.
145. It follows from the above, and the Board of Appeal therefore concludes, that the documentary evidence
concerning the representation of the legal entities in this case did raise clear concerns and doubts, and
was not sufficiently up-to-date or complete, to permit the CPVO to register the transfer.
146. As regards the documentation concerning the transfer of the PVR itself, the Board of Appeal takes the
view that it is important for the CPVO to ensure that the documentary evidence does not raise clear
concerns and doubts regarding the transfer of the PVR. In this regard, the Board of Appeal notes, amongst
other provisions, the requirement for “official documents confirming the transfer, or of such extracts from
those documents as suffice to establish the transfer” set out at Article 79 of the IRR. Moreover, the Board
of Appeal notes the CPVO regarding “Assignments - Validity”9 which states:
147. In this case, from the documentation made available to the Board of Appeal, it appears that there are
legitimate grounds supporting the view that the documentary evidence raises concerns and doubts
regarding the transfer of the Queen PVR. That is because – and without it being necessary to assess this
issue in detail given the conclusions already drawn above – from the documentary evidence provided, the
Board of Appeal notes that the wording contained in the 3-paged document referred to at paragraph [5]
above and concerning the transfer the Queen PVR could, on its face, be construed as insufficiently clear,
precise and/or unambiguous to permit registration of a transfer. The Board of Appeal notes in particular
that some of the wording contained within the 3-paged document could, for example, be construed as
transferring exploitation rights of the Queen PVR, rather than the Queen PVR itself. Without the Board of
9 https://cpvo.europa.eu/sites/default/files/documents/assignment_note_en_0.pdf
23

Appeal in any way opining or taking a view on whether or not the Queen PVR was transferred to SGI in
this case, the Board of Appeal merely notes that, pursuant to the current rules and guidance incumbent
on the CPVO, the relevant wording in the transfer document should be sufficiently clear to enable the
CPVO to register the transfer of a PVR. In the absence of documents which properly establish the existence
of a transfer, there is no justification for the CPVO to register a transfer (see, by analogy, Order of the
General Court of 7 November 2023, Sattvica SA v European Union Intellectual Property Office (EUIPO),
Case T-299/22, ECLI:EU:T:2023:710, paragraphs 39-43).
148. It follows from the above, and the Board of Appeal therefore concludes, that there are legitimate grounds
supporting the view that the documentary evidence raises concerns and doubts regarding the transfer of
the Queen PVR and the wording in the documentary evidence could be construed as insufficient to permit
the CPVO to register the transfer.
149. In light of the above, the Board of Appeal concludes that the documentary evidence in place was
insufficient to permit the registration of the transfer in this case.
150. In addition to the pleas and arguments summarised above, the Board of Appeal notes that the CPVO has
also brought forward arguments asserting that, in this case, insofar as the Appellant company ceased or
withdrew its appointment of Mr Renán as a representative – the legal onus and burden was on the
Appellant company to “immediately” inform the CPVO of this issue. According to the CPVO, the CPVO
cannot be held responsible for registering the transfer of the PVR right due to a failure of the Appellant
company to immediately inform the CPVO of the cessation or withdrawal of Mr Renán’s permission to
represent the Appellant company. The CPVO has referred to Article 73(4) of the IRR which states that “A
procedural representative whose mandate has ended shall continue to be considered as procedural
representative until the termination of his mandate has been communicated to the Office.”
151. The Board of Appeal notes the following points.
152. Firstly, and as stated at paragraph [143] above, from the documentary evidence made available to the
Board of Appeal, it is not apparent that the Appellant company has ever appointed Mr Renán as a
representative before the CPVO as regards procedures involving the registration of the transfer of the
Queen PVR to third parties.
153. Secondly, Article 73(4) of the IRR relates to ‘procedural representatives’ within the meaning of Article 82
of the Basic Regulation. As is clear from Article 82 of the Basic Regulation, ‘procedural representatives’
are a specific type of representative established by the legislature which are capable of being appointed
in specific situations. The legal concept of a ‘procedural representative’ under Article 82 of the Basic
Regulation may be comparable to, for example, ‘only representatives’ appointed pursuant to Article 8 of
Regulation 1907/2006.
154. As such, the provisions concerning procedural representatives do not per se relate, and were not intended
to relate, to all natural persons appointed to act on behalf of a legal entity. Those provisions cannot de
facto, and automatically apply to every situation involving the appointment of a natural person to
represent a legal person (e.g. a company) in proceedings involving the CPVO. Pursuant to the principles
of good administration, legal certainty and legitimate expectation, it is generally for the CPVO to state
clearly how the CPVO will take proportionate steps to confirm natural persons have been appointed to
represent a legal person in proceedings involving the CPVO. Documents provided by the CPVO concerning
the representation of legal persons before the CPVO – should be clear and precise, and should not lead
to confusion vis-à-vis the appointment and obligations incumbent on ‘procedural representatives’.
155. Thirdly, to the extent that the CPVO asserts the CPVO was right to consider Mr Renán as a representative
of the Appellant company as regards the transfer of the Queen PVR, as the Appellant company failed to
“immediately” inform the CPVO that Mr Renán was no longer its representative – the Board of Appeal
notes that this argument cannot succeed. It is settled case-law that: “…Community legislation must be
certain and its application foreseeable by individuals. The principle of legal certainty requires that every
measure of the institutions having legal effects must be clear and precise and must be brought to the
notice of the person concerned in such a way that he can ascertain exactly the time at which the measure
comes into being and starts to have legal effects” (emphasis added) (See the Judgment of the Court of
First Instance of 22 January 1997, Opel Austria v Council, Case T-115/94 ECR II-39, ECLI:EU:T:1997:3,
paragraph 124 and the case-law cited therein). If the CPVO wishes to pursue a policy requiring titleholders
to “immediately” inform the CPVO of the cessation of an individual’s legal powers of representation, it is
necessary for the CPVO to inform stakeholders of this policy in accordance with the principles of good
administration, legal certainty and legitimate expectation. The CPVO is therefore bound to bring this to
24

the attention and notice of stakeholders, and the legality of such a policy, including its implementation,
would be considered in light of, amongst other things, the principle of proportionality. The CPVO has
not brought forward any sufficient evidence that the CPVO has or is informing and warning stakeholders
of this policy. The Board of Appeal can find no relevant reference in the guidance or in the current template
forms, advising or warning stakeholders to “immediately” inform the CPVO if and when they cancel,
withdraw, or revoke an individual’s ability to represent them.
156. In light of the above, the Board of Appeal concludes that the CPVO’s arguments asserting that at the time
it registered the transfer, the CPVO was right to assume Mr Renán acted as the valid representative of
the Appellant company as regards the registration of the transfer – cannot succeed.
157. On the basis of the initial conclusions and other points set out above, in particular the applicability of the
duty of diligence, and the inability for the CPVO’s arguments to succeed in this context, the Board of
Appeal concludes that: the CPVO did not act in accordance with the duty of diligence, the CPVO failed to
fully discharge its duty of diligence before registering the transfer, and, as such, the CPVO acted in breach
of that duty. This constitutes a relevant error in the appraisal of the facts and law, and a breach of a rule
of law. The Board of Appeal therefore concludes that this plea is well founded and should be upheld.
158. Without it being necessary to assess the other pleas in law, the Board of Appeal therefore concludes that
the act of registering the transfer of the Queen PVR from the Appellant to SGI on the Register, is vitiated
by a manifest error of EU law and must therefore be vacated.
VI. DECISION AND STATEMENT REGARDING APPEAL
159. The CPVO Board of Appeal orders that:
(i) The appeal be upheld.
(ii) The decision to delete and enter certain information on the Register concerning the Queen PVR to
indicate SGI as the Titleholder and not the Appellant be annulled and vacated.
(iii) The relevant details in the Register concerning the Queen PVR be amended so as to indicate that the
Appellant is the Titleholder.
(iv) The relevant details in the Register concerning the Queen PVR contain a statement referring to this
decision and the pending legal proceedings before the Spanish Courts.
(v) In the event that this decision is appealed, a relevant reference is made in the Register.
(vi) The CPVO reimburse SGI for the costs of registering the transfer.
(vii) The CPVO reimburse SGI the annual fees and/or other relevant costs.
(viii) The CPVO reimburse the Appellant the appeal fee relating to this appeal case.
(ix) The CPVO take the necessary steps for the Appellant company to pay the relevant costs and fees as
maybe necessary as from 16 December 2021, or earlier. In the event the Appellant fails to pay the
necessary costs and fees as necessary, the CPVO take relevant action.
(x) The Board of Appeal leaves it for the parties in these proceedings to communicate a copy of this
decision to the relevant Member State national courts and authorities.
160. Pursuant to Article 73 of the Basic Regulation, an action may be brought before the Court of Justice
against this decision, within two months of its service.
